speaker: interviewer
text: Today is July 28th, 1996. And we are in Portland, Maine. Tama, you are describing your adjustments to American life as a 12-year-old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're learning English. Would you say it was a-- a difficult experience, or pleasant or unpleasant?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult, in the fact that English is very difficult. Writing it is even more difficult. [LAUGHS] But like I said, I had wonderful teachers. The kids in the class were very good to me. They-- I started to say before, as we were going up the stairs, one of the kids caught the back-- back of my heel. And it-- and I just turned and said excuse me, the excitement was so tremendous. Because that was the first time they heard me utter an English word. They ran to the teacher and they told her that I spoke. I mean, it was like, unbelievable. Tama spoke. I haven't stopped talking since. I tell you, that was the beginning-- [LAUGHS] --of my English.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Did you ever find yourself thinking about what you had been through and seeing how other children were leave-- living? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That whole time, it was an awful lot of sleepless nights, the horror and the terror of the dead bodies lying in the woods, lying in the field, lying on the streets. Just people, dead, lying there. I had a very difficult time. I couldn't get it out of my head many times. I tried. As time went on, I tried pushing it away and pushing it away. As a matter of fact, they wanted me to speak in schools and stuff like that, about my experiences. And I couldn't do it. It was very difficult for me. I couldn't seem to go back. Because of so many years of trying to get it out of my head, I had a lot of nightmares. I would wake up screaming. [SIGHS] Chasing, I was always being chased. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I didn't want to relive it. I really didn't. I just thought if I stopped thinking about it, I wouldn't have it in my head anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't go see movies that had Germans in it. To this day, when I see that helmet, I get goosebumps up and down my skin. It just crawls. That horrible sound they had when they had like, a siren sound, that sound was always in my head. And it scared me. Horrible, horrible pictures that were coming through my head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people ask you to speak about it? Friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the schools. Yes, we have a lot of people that go before a school, which I give them all the credit in the world. It has to be told. The story has to be told. I know it does. My grandchildren, I think, are learning about it as we speak, probably, a lot of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your children about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they heard most of it from my. Mother like I said, I was not one to open up too much about it. But my mother did. I just wish to God she was here in my place. Because she-- she is the one, she's the heroine. She fought so hard. And she pulled me through. She would never let go of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she talk about her experiences to her relatives? Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They spoke about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how was their reaction to her stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unbelievable, that anybody could survive and go through such an ordeal. I know the people that had a terrible time in concentration camps, we heard about them. Because like I told you at the beginning, we saw the trucks, when they piled the people on the trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we heard about them. We know what terrible things they were doing to them. And this is why our run was always increased.

speaker: interviewee
text: You gotta keep running. You can't stop. You have to run. You have to be strong. You have to carry on. And that's all I ever knew. Thirst, that hunger. You know, to this day, some of my friends think I'm crazy when I go shopping. I buy 10 times more than I need. It's like my security blanket. I don't want to run out of food. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? At age 59, I'm afraid I'm going to run out of food. God knows I don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But it's-- it's-- it's left in there. It is. It's-- What was your mother's life like here, after the war? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's life was hard, in the fact that she had to work again. My stepfather made very little money. He-- they got him a job in a lumber place, where he was a night watchman. And he was making $26 a week. And my mother went to work as making her specialties in a restaurant, in a deli place. And she always had these knishes and blintzes, Jewish specialties, you know, with the kreplach and stuff like that. She went in there and she says, you don't have those things here. Let me be the one to make it. And you'll see they're going to sell. And that's how she started. And this is what she did. And she continued to do that for a long time. And then she went with catering, a caterer here in the city of Portland. And she worked for her for 27 years. My mother made our wedding. And in turn, my mother and I and my friends made the bar mitzvah of our son, Larry. I wish he was here today. But he had to be out of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, Abe, and I were married October 23rd, 1955, in the city of Portland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was born-- born right here in Portland. He's a maniac. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's-- what's his reaction to your talking about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely supportive. Extremely. He was the one that always wanted me to go. And too, he felt it was important for me to give my story to a lot of the school that called me and wanted me to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother get together with other survivors and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. They had a whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would that be like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was wonderful. It was a-- a joy and celebration every time. She was very hospitable. And she always had her house open. They-- she continued her kosher when she came back. And she said, if God gave me my life and my child, the least I can do is go back to my heritage of keeping kosher. And the belief in God came back. See, I had a trouble with that. But she went right back. And they used to have a lot of-- and they still do-- a lot of-- the Hasidim would come to town to try and get some money. And then they needed a place to stay that was kosher. And it was always my mother's house that they stayed at. And she fed them and they all knew Mrs. Bichman will always have the door open.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- what difficulty did you have with the religious parts?

speaker: interviewee
text: It always bothered me if there is a God, how could he allow a little tiny six-month-old baby being ripped apart? Which is what we saw in the woods. Or what a saw, cut in half, and just limp, thrown about?

speaker: interviewee
text: How could a God do that? Where was that God? What did I do to anyone that I had to go through this kind of a life? And that was a hard thing for me, to get that through. Now that 50 years have passed since the Shoah-- --is there anything that you would like to tell your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I want my children to remember that I'm doing this. [CRYING] I have to put this on. Because I didn't know if I would be able to be composed enough to say it. 'That I hope that this small contribution of mine will help dispute the deniers, that there was no Holocaust.' I assure you, there was a terrible Holocaust. And so many lives were taken so needlessly. So for my children and grandchildren, don't be afraid. And don't deny your heritage. You have a rich, strong heritage. You're Jewish. Keep that heritage going. And I hope that those lives that perished will not be for nothing. That a little bit, my mother said, it's a tree. And you're just a part-- a limb on this tree. Well, my children and my grandchildren they're the other little limb. And hopefully from that limb, a strong oak will grow. And we can continue. We have to be good to one another. This terrible brutality has got to stop. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] At this time, it gives me a great pleasure to introduce my husband, Abe Fineberg, to my right. This is my daughter-in-law, [? Suzanne ?] Fineberg. My daughter, Debbie Fineberg-Finn, my grandchildren. This is David Finn. This is Lauren Fineberg. This is Benjamin Finn. I'm sorry that my son, Larry Fineberg, and my grandson, Morgan, are unable to be here. This is my beautiful family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: ABRAHAM (HUSBAND): I just want to say, I married this beautiful woman 40 years ago. And she's as beautiful today as she was then. [LAUGHS] I'm grateful that she survived the Holocaust. I'm also grateful that she survived a bout with cancer, and that she could be here today to be on this program. And I love her very much. I think that's all. DEBBIE (DAUGHTER): OK, well, I wrote a poem. [LAUGHS] 'I grew up in Portland, Maine. Little did I know we weren't the same. A happy child in every way, I would learn the truth one sad day. Mother, father, brother too, taught to be proud to be a Jew. Wonderful grandparents and Bubbie Rose, I never knew the cruel fate life chose. As I grew older and the truth be known, I was amazed at the strength that I'd been shown. Mom's migraine headaches and sleepless nights, she had demons to fight. And that was her right. So many times I've asked why. So many times I have cried.

speaker: interviewee
text: If only I could have spared her some pain. Why were so many slain? As the years go by, I still ask why. I'll never understand, as hard as I try.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm older now. And I have to say, I'm one of the lucky ones. I have my mom with me today. If I had one dream that I could make true, this is what I'd love to do. To buy my mom a childhood, that would be my dream, if I only could. She never knew a carefree life, only pain and sorrow, hardship and strife. Surviving is all my mom ever knew. I can't believe the pain that she went through. Although I have known the hell she went through, I'm sure there were times that she didn't know if the words were not there when they should've been, she's the only mom I'd wish for if I could do it again. I'm glad that her story is now being told. Hopefully it will create some peace as she grows old. This story should make it to the big screen. No one could describe what my mom has seen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mr. Spielberg, I'd just like to say, if you make this a movie, playing me, Marisa Tomei. I have to thank you for all the work you have done. Mrs. Spielberg raised a very nice son.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is part of history, painful and true. It should not be forgotten, what so many went through. Letting go of the pain, gaining strength on the way, it makes you appreciate life every day. So whoever in a million years, through all the happiness and tears, would find us all here today with so much there is to say about my mom-- [CRYING] --my best friend, a survivor, a fighter to the very end. I'm the most fortunate, above no other. Because I have the privilege to call her my mother.' Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE] BENJAMIN (GRANDSON): I wrote an acrostic poem for my Grammy Tammy. ABRAHAM (HUSBAND): Wow. BENJAMIN (GRANDSON): G stands for Great chicken soup. [LAUGHTER] R stands for Really nice. A stands for Awesome meatloaf. M stands for my mommy's mommy. M stands for Mighty fine. Y stands for Young looking. T stands for taking-- Takes care of us when we are sick. A stands for Always loves us. M stands for makes me laugh. M stands for Makes me happy. And Y stands for Yummy food. LAUREN (GRANDDAUGHTER): Oh-- BENJAMIN (GRANDSON): And altogether, that spells my grandmother, Grammy Tammy. DAVID (GRANDSON): I made a poem. And this is what I said. 'If I didn't have Grammy, I wouldn't have tasted the greatest chicken soup. If I didn't have Grammy, I wouldn't have tasted Grammy's meatloaf. If I didn't have Grammy, I wouldn't even be alive. If I didn't have Grammy, I would have never been to Maine. If I didn't have Grammy, I would never find out how much you loved me. If I didn't have Grammy, I wouldn't love someone any more than I love her. [CRYING] Thank you. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: DEBBIE (DAUGHTER): And Lauren? Lauren, what do you have to say about Grammy? Hmm? You love your Grammy, right? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my beautiful family, for which I am extremely grateful for. And I thank God. I've come back to God. [LAUGHS] And I thank him every day. And I only wish my mother, Rose-- [CRYING] --was here, with all of us. She had so much to say. And I miss her every day. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Spielberg. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do I get the pictures? Where are the pictures? DEBBIE (DAUGHTER): [INAUDIBLE]. On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I'd like to thank you for inviting us to your home, for introducing us to your family, and for sharing with us the stories of your survival, and of your mother's experiences, and your whole family. Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Chaya. I appreciate you being here. This is the picture-- the only picture that I have of my oldest sister, Malla. That was taken in my town that we were born in, Mlynow. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This little picture-- and I'm in that little corner there. I don't know if you can see me or not. But that was taken around 1945. And it was May Day in Russia, which happens to be my birthday, May 1st. And I thought they were having a party for me. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERS) What do I have on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the only picture I have of myself at a young age. And I must have been about eight years old then. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is of my mother and myself. We were in the Displaced Persons camp. And they set up a special place for a lot of the children that were malnourished. And I was one of them. But my mother, she couldn't stay away. She came to stay with me. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is a picture that was taken in Pucking, Germany, a picture of my best friend's sister. It's an invitation. She turned 16 years old in Pucking, Germany. And also being a survivor, her parents wanted to celebrate the happy occasion. So they drew this picture. And as you can see, the two arms ripping apart that horrible German cross. And the Star of David, making us human beings again. And I've always kept it for memories. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This picture is of myself and my girlfriend, taken in Pucking, Germany. And the two of us were walking during the daytime, like regular people. It was good to be out. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] This picture was taken, once again, in Pucking, Germany. And it was what they formed, a kibbutz, a group for a lot of the kids who lost their mothers and fathers, a lot of the orphans. I was put in that group because in hopes that we would go to Israel, being a part of it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] This is a picture of my mother, the lady that made my life possible. [CRYING] She is the one without whom I would never be here today. She went through fire and everything, just to save me. And she did. [CRYING] I wish she was here today. She could tell you a lot of things. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is a picture of my stepfather, David Bichman. He, too, was a Holocaust survivor. He lost his entire family. And after the terrible, horrendous Holocaust, he and my mother got together. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is a picture of my son, Larry Fineberg, who was named after my father, Leib, and my daughter, Debbie, and my mother. Those two children were her pride and joy. [CRYING] And she just loved them so much. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] (WHISPERS) It was a wedding. Oh, God. I think I screwed it up. I didn't say it was-- I didn't mention at the beginning, my daughter Debbie, that was the day that she got married. And my son was an usher. And of course, my mother, in the middle of her two pride and joys, Larry and Debbie.
